### Table of the top findings

#### Across-group

##### Before treatment

-   Multiple testing correction: **FDR**
-   Statistical test: **Wilcoxon**






::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

\begin{longtable}[t]{lrrrrl}
\caption{\label{tab:table-of-findings-before}Table of alpha diversity comparisons before Treatment}\\
\toprule
\textbf{group1} & \textbf{group2} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.adj}\\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\caption[]{Table of alpha diversity comparisons before Treatment \textit{(continued)}}\\
\toprule
\textbf{group1} & \textbf{group2} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.adj}\\
\midrule
\endhead

\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
oat & rice & 210.9 & 231.3 & 0.13 & 0.133\\*
\end{longtable}


:::
:::






Before treatment, the rice group had a slightly higher observed alpha diversity `(231.30)` compared to the oat group `(210.90)`. The fold change (log2FC) of `0.13` suggests a small increase in richness for the rice group. However, the corrected p-value `(p.adj = 0.133)` indicates that this difference is not statistically significant, meaning it could be due to random variation rather than a true effect of diet.

##### After treatment

-   Multiple testing correction: **FDR**
-   Statistical test: **Wilcoxon**







::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

\begin{longtable}[t]{lrrrrl}
\caption{\label{tab:table-of-findings-after}Table of alpha diversity comparisons after Treatment}\\
\toprule
\textbf{group1} & \textbf{group2} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.adj}\\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\caption[]{Table of alpha diversity comparisons after Treatment \textit{(continued)}}\\
\toprule
\textbf{group1} & \textbf{group2} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.adj}\\
\midrule
\endhead

\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
oat & rice & 206.9 & 233 & 0.17 & 0.054\\*
\end{longtable}


:::
:::








After treatment, the rice group `(233.00)` continued to show a higher observed alpha diversity compared to the oat group `(206.90)`, with a fold change (log2FC) of `0.17`. The adjusted p-value `(p.adj = 0.0540)` is approaching statistical significance, suggesting a potential effect of diet on diversity, though it remains marginal.






::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](alpha_observed_files/figure-pdf/viz-div_change-1.pdf){fig-align='center'}
:::
:::






#### Within-group

##### Paired treatment

-   Multiple testing correction: **paired** -none
-   Statistical test: **Wilcoxon**






::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

\begin{longtable}[t]{lrrrr}
\caption{\label{tab:table-of-findings-paired}Table of alpha diversity paired between timepoint(1 and 2)}\\
\toprule
\textbf{diet} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.value}\\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\caption[]{Table of alpha diversity paired between timepoint(1 and 2) \textit{(continued)}}\\
\toprule
\textbf{diet} & \textbf{mean1} & \textbf{mean2} & \textbf{log2FC} & \textbf{p.value}\\
\midrule
\endhead

\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
oat & 210.9 & 206.9 & 0.04 & 0.120\\
rice & 231.3 & 233.0 & 0.13 & 0.363\\*
\end{longtable}


:::
:::








Oat group: Observed alpha diversity slightly decreased from `210.90` to `206.90` (log2FC = `0.04`, p = `0.120`), indicating a small but non-significant reduction over time.

Rice group: Diversity increased modestly from `231.30` to `233.00` (log2FC = `0.13`, p = `0.363`), but this change is also not statistically significant.

Overall, there were no significant within-group changes in observed alpha diversity over time, suggesting that dietary intervention had a minimal effect on diversity shifts within each group.

### LMM






::: {.cell}

:::






-   Multiple testing correction: **FDR**
-   Statistical test: **paired Wilcoxon**
-   Formula used: **~ diet * timepoint + (1 | id)**






::: {.cell}

:::

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

\begin{longtable}[t]{lrrrrl}
\caption{\label{tab:table-fx}Table of interaction effects}\\
\toprule
\textbf{Term} & \textbf{Estimate} & \textbf{Std. Error} & \textbf{df} & \textbf{t value} & \textbf{Pr(>|t|)}\\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\caption[]{Table of interaction effects \textit{(continued)}}\\
\toprule
\textbf{Term} & \textbf{Estimate} & \textbf{Std. Error} & \textbf{df} & \textbf{t value} & \textbf{Pr(>|t|)}\\
\midrule
\endhead

\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
(Intercept) & 7.672 & 0.084 & 88.336 & 90.944 & 0.000\\
dietrice & 0.115 & 0.118 & 88.333 & 0.973 & 0.333\\
time & -0.027 & 0.020 & 67.103 & -1.365 & 0.177\\
dietrice:time & 0.036 & 0.028 & 67.102 & 1.291 & 0.201\\*
\end{longtable}


:::
:::








Diet effect (rice vs. oat): No significant difference between rice and oat groups `(Estimate = 0.12, p = 0.333`), suggesting baseline diversity was similar.

Time effect: A slight decrease in diversity over time `(Estimate = -0.03, p = 0.177)`, but this was not statistically significant.

Diet × Time interaction: The interaction term `(Estimate = 0.04, p = 0.201)` suggests that the change in diversity over time did not significantly differ between the oat and rice groups.

## Alternate analyses






::: {.cell}

:::

::: {.cell}

:::






-   **Beta (Estimate)**: Shows the effect size and direction of a predictor on the outcome. Positive means an increase, negative a decrease.

-   **95% CI**: Range where the true beta is likely to fall, reflecting precision.

-   **p-Value**: Tests if beta differs from zero; p\<0.05p \< 0.05p\<0.05 suggests significance.
